data.title: Is Veralto’s Mixed Quarter and Softer EPS Outlook Altering The Investment Case For VLTO?
data.description:

  • This combination of revenue growth, an earnings beat, and more cautious forward EPS guidance highlights how Veralto is balancing current demand with a measured outlook on profitability.

Veralto Investment Narrative Recap

To own Veralto, you have to believe in long term demand for water analytics and treatment and in the company’s ability to turn that demand into resilient, growing cash flows. The latest quarter’s 3.8% year on year revenue increase to US$1.40 billion and EPS beat support that case, while the softer next quarter EPS guidance reminds me that margin pressure, especially around costs and China exposure, still looks like the key near term risk. Overall, this update does not materially change that balance.

The most relevant recent announcement, in my view, is management’s February 2026 reaffirmation of an active M&A pipeline in Water Quality and Product Quality & Innovation, backed by solid free cash flow and a conservative balance sheet. When I put that alongside the current mix of revenue growth and cautious EPS guidance, it reinforces the idea that bolt on deals and software driven offerings could be important catalysts for future earnings, but also that integration costs and execution risk remain front of mind.

Yet behind the steady growth story, investors should be aware of the margin risk if rising input costs cannot be fully passed through...

data.title: Baudroieinc (TSE:4413) Earnings Growth Steady At 36.6% Reinforces Bullish Narratives
data.description:
baudroieinc (TSE:4413) has just closed FY 2026 with fourth quarter revenue of ¥5.1b and basic EPS of ¥24.23, capping a year in which trailing 12 month revenue reached ¥17.4b and EPS came in at ¥79.07. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from ¥3.2b in FY 2025 Q4 to ¥5.1b in FY 2026 Q4, while basic EPS moved from ¥17.29 to ¥24.23 over the same period. This gives investors a clear line of sight on how the top and bottom lines have tracked through the year. With trailing net margin at 14.1%, the latest results put the focus on how durable that profitability profile looks against the growth story.

See our full analysis for baudroieinc.

With the headline numbers set, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up with the main stories around baudroieinc, and where the hard data pushes back on those narratives.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

36.6% earnings growth sets the tone

  • Over the last 12 months, net income reached ¥2,457 million and basic EPS on this basis was ¥79.07, compared with ¥1,799 million and ¥56.68 a year earlier, which lines up with the 36.6% trailing earnings growth figure.
  • Supporters of a bullish view often point to this 36.6% earnings growth and the move in trailing revenue from ¥11,649 million to ¥17,423 million as evidence of a business that is scaling. However, the step up in quarterly net income from ¥543.18 million in FY 2025 Q4 to ¥754.96 million in FY 2026 Q4 also means investors need to watch how consistently that level can be maintained.
    • Back to back quarters in FY 2026 show net income at ¥437.43 million, ¥576.57 million, ¥687.24 million and then ¥754.96 million, which fits a growth story but also raises the bar for future comparisons.
    • With trailing EPS now at ¥79.07, the bullish angle that the business has more earnings power than a year ago is grounded in hard numbers, not just forecasts.

Fans of balanced analysis who want to see how these growth numbers fit into a wider company view can check what other investors are focusing on through the 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about baudroieinc.

Margins hold at 14.1% as scale builds

  • Trailing net margin sits at 14.1% on ¥17,423 million of revenue, compared with 15.4% on ¥11,649 million a year earlier, so profit is higher in absolute yen terms even though each yen of sales now brings in a slightly smaller share of profit.
  • What stands out against a bullish angle is that while revenue over the trailing year rose from ¥11,649 million to ¥17,423 million and net income climbed from ¥1,799 million to ¥2,457 million, the margin slip from 15.4% to 14.1% shows that part of the growth story is coming from volume rather than fatter margins.
    • Quarterly data for FY 2026, with revenue running between ¥3,361.91 million and ¥5,099.47 million and net income between ¥437.43 million and ¥754.96 million, points to higher profit but not to widening margins in the latest snapshot.
    • Bulls who focus on revenue and EPS growth get support from the higher trailing totals, while the margin move gives a concrete number to track for quality of that growth.

P/E premium and DCF gap create valuation tension

  • At a share price of ¥2,277, baudroieinc trades on a trailing P/E of 28.9x against a peer average of 24.4x and a Japan IT industry average of 14.5x, yet the DCF fair value is set at ¥2,776.70, which is about 22% above the current price.
  • Critics who take a more bearish line often highlight the 28.9x P/E premium over peers and the move in net margin from 15.4% to 14.1% as signs that investors are paying up for growth that still has to be earned, while supporters point to the roughly ¥500 gap between the ¥2,277 market price and the ¥2,776.70 DCF fair value as a potential cushion.
    • The combination of 36.6% trailing earnings growth and forecasts calling for around 25.21% earnings growth and 27% revenue growth per year helps explain why the share trades above peer and sector P/E levels.
    • At the same time, recent share price volatility above the wider Japan market means any disappointment on growth or margins could matter more for holders who paid that premium multiple.

Next Steps

Don't just look at this quarter; the real story is in the long-term trend. We've done an in-depth analysis on baudroieinc's growth and its valuation to see if today's price is a bargain. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio now so you don't miss the next big move.

data.title: Reassessing NuScale Power (SMR) After Recent Share Price Swings And DCF Valuation Results
data.description:
• Wondering whether NuScale Power's current share price really reflects what you are getting for your money? This breakdown aims to help you weigh up the stock's value with clear, structured checks.
  • NuScale Power recently closed at US$11.71, with returns of 18.8% over the last 7 days, a 2.2% decline over 30 days, a 28.2% decline year to date, and a 19.2% decline over 1 year, set against gains of 32.3% over 3 years and 16.9% over 5 years.
  • These mixed returns have kept NuScale Power in focus, as investors reassess how the share price lines up with the fundamentals and market expectations. Recent coverage has highlighted the stock's volatility and raised questions about whether current levels properly reflect the company's long term prospects.
  • NuScale Power currently scores 1 out of 6 on our valuation checks. The next sections will walk through what different valuation approaches say about the stock and point to an even better way to think about value at the end of the article.

NuScale Power scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: NuScale Power Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today, using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all those future dollars are worth in current terms.

For NuScale Power, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $460.39 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections show free cash flow staying negative in the next few years, then reaching $103.66 million by 2030, with further projected figures continuing to rise after that based on Simply Wall St estimates.

Pulling all of those projected cash flows together and discounting them back, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of $7.96 per share. Compared with the recent market price of $11.71, this implies the shares are about 47.1% above the model’s estimate of fair value. This points to a rich valuation on this cash flow view.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests NuScale Power may be overvalued by 47.1%. Discover 60 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for NuScale Power.

Approach 2: NuScale Power Price vs Book

For companies where profits are limited or still developing, the price to book ratio is often more useful than earnings based metrics because it compares the share price with the value of net assets on the balance sheet.

In general, the higher the expected growth and the lower the perceived risk, the higher the multiple investors may be willing to pay, while slower growth or higher risk tends to justify a lower, more conservative multiple.

NuScale Power currently trades on a P/B of 3.19x. That sits above the Electrical industry average P/B of 2.77x and below the peer group average of 10.55x, so simple comparisons send a mixed signal about how stretched the valuation is relative to others.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is designed to give a cleaner read than peer or sector comparisons alone. It estimates the P/B multiple that might be reasonable given factors such as earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks, rather than assuming all Electrical companies deserve similar valuations.

In this case, there is no Fair Ratio available, so it is not possible to say whether NuScale Power screens as overvalued, undervalued or about right on this metric.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Approach 1: Navan Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projected future cash flows, then discounts them back into today’s dollars to estimate what the business might be worth right now. It is essentially asking what future streams of cash are worth in the present.

For Navan, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $11.20 million. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/S depending on what they expect for future growth and how risky they believe those revenues are.

Navan trades on a P/S of about 5.15x. The Hospitality industry average P/S is around 1.57x, and the peer group used here sits at roughly 1.74x, so Navan is priced at a higher multiple of sales than both of those benchmarks.

Approach 1: BNP Paribas Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a bank can generate over and above the return that equity investors typically require, and then links that to the value of its shares.

For BNP Paribas, the model works off a book value of €104.44 per share and a stable earnings per share estimate of €13.23, based on weighted future Return on Equity inputs from 15 analysts. The average Return on Equity used in the model is 11.14%, compared with a calculated cost of equity of €8.77 per share. That gap feeds into an excess return of €4.47 per share, which is then applied to a stable book value estimate of €118.77 per share, itself based on book value estimates from 9 analysts.

Running these inputs through the Excess Returns framework gives an intrinsic value estimate of €206.26 per share. Against the current share price of €91.37, this implies a 55.7% discount, which indicates that the shares screen as materially undervalued on this method.

Approach 2: BNP Paribas Price vs Earnings

For a profitable bank like BNP Paribas, the P/E ratio is a practical yardstick because it links what you pay per share to the earnings that each share generates. It helps you see how much the market is willing to pay for current earnings, given the company’s profile.

What counts as a “normal” P/E ratio tends to reflect two things: how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how much risk investors see in those earnings.   • What bullish investors highlight is that this combination of a sub 90% combined ratio and 33.6% earnings growth is exactly what they point to when arguing Progressive’s pricing and telematics capabilities can support strong profitability. The bearish narrative, however, counters that climate and cost inflation risks could still pressure that 87.4% combined ratio if claims volatility rises from here.
    • The bullish side leans on Progressive’s use of data and rapid rate adjustments to argue that underwriting margins can stay attractive even when costs move, which lines up with the improved net margin implied by the latest 12.9% figure.
    • Skeptics focus on the risk section that flags rising repair and healthcare costs plus weather events as threats to loss ratios, using those points to argue that today’s 87.4% combined ratio might sit above where they would like to see it in a more stressed claims environment.

Bulls argue this margin profile could underpin further upside if pricing power holds, while skeptics are watching closely for any cracks in underwriting quality before they give the all clear on the profit story. 🐂 Progressive Bull Case

Valuation Gap Versus DCF Fair Value

  • Progressive trades around US$201 per share while the provided DCF fair value is US$458.65, so the stock is referenced as sitting roughly 56% below that DCF fair value even though the trailing P/E of 10.4x is a bit higher than the 9.0x peer average and lower than the 11.6x US Insurance industry average.
  • Critics highlight that, despite this apparent discount to DCF fair value, the same dataset shows analysts expect earnings to decline by about 0.9% per year over the next three years, which they argue makes it harder to justify Progressive trading well above peer P/E levels for long.
    • The bearish narrative leans on that forecast earnings decline and the mention of margin compression risk to argue the current 10.4x P/E might already be fair if profitability normalizes, even with the large headline gap to DCF fair value.
    • At the same time, the 33.6% trailing earnings growth and 12.9% net margin are used by more optimistic investors as evidence that the market might be underweighting recent execution, so there is a clear tension between the backward looking strength and the forward looking caution baked into those forecasts.

Skeptics warn that the combination of a forecast earnings decline and higher than peer P/E could limit how much of that DCF gap actually closes, making it important for you to stress test whether the current US$201 price already reflects more cautious profit expectations. 🐻 Progressive Bear Case

Revenue Growth Trails Market, Earnings Outpace

  • Over the last year, Progressive’s revenue grew 6.7% while earnings grew 33.6%, so profit growth has outpaced the top line even though the 6.7% revenue growth trails the 10.8% US market benchmark cited in the data.
  • Consensus narrative notes that Progressive’s technology driven distribution and telematics give it an edge in winning and retaining customers. However, the fact that revenue is growing slower than the broader market while margins improve suggests the recent 33.6% earnings growth has leaned more on profitability than on breakaway premium growth.
    • On one hand, the stronger margin profile and 12.9% net margin support the idea that analytics and pricing discipline are adding value, which is consistent with the consensus view that Progressive operates efficiently.
    • On the other hand, the 6.7% revenue growth versus a 10.8% benchmark shows why some investors may question how durable the current earnings pace is if volume growth stays below that wider market reference.
